title: Broken Access Control
description: Access control enforces policy such that users cannot act outside of their intended permissions. Failures typically lead to unauthorized information disclosure, modification, or destruction of all data or performing a business function outside of the user's limits.
real_world_example: An application allows a user to access another user's account details by simply changing the 'account_id' parameter in the URL (Insecure Direct Object Reference).
impact: Exploitation can lead to full system compromise, data leakage of sensitive PII, and unauthorized administrative actions.
prevention: Implement the Principle of Least Privilege. Employ centralized access control modules. Deny by default. Use unique IDs (UUIDs) instead of sequential integers in URLs.
defensive_strategy: Zero Trust Architecture, Mandatory Access Control (MAC), and regular permission audits.
secure_coding_checklist: Check permissions at the server-side for every request.
Use cryptographically strong identifiers for sensitive resources.
Ensure administrative interfaces are isolated and require multi-factor authentication.
real_attack_scenario: Threat actor gains access to a standard user account and uses a flawed API endpoint to escalate privileges to 'Super Admin' by modifying a JWT claim.

title: Security Misconfiguration
description: Security misconfiguration can happen at any level of an application stack, including the network, platform, web server, application server, framework, and custom code.
real_world_example: A cloud storage bucket containing customer backups is accidentally set to 'Public Read', allowing anyone on the internet to download sensitive data.
impact: Exposes sensitive data, provides attackers with a foothold into the internal network, and allows unauthorized administrative access.
prevention: Automate configuration hardening. Remove unnecessary features and default accounts. Use Infrastructure as Code (IaC) with security linting.
defensive_strategy: Continuous Security Monitoring, Compliance as Code, and automated vulnerability scanning.
secure_coding_checklist: Disable directory listing on web servers.
Remove all default passwords and accounts during deployment.
Implement strong Security Headers (CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options).
real_attack_scenario: Attacker identifies an application running with 'Debug Mode' enabled, exposing environment variables including database credentials in error messages.

title: Software Supply Chain Failures
description: Failures in the integrity of the software supply chain, including the use of modules from untrusted sources, compromised build systems, or insecure update mechanisms.
real_world_example: A popular open-source library used for data processing is found to have had a 'typosquatted' malicious twin uploaded to a public repository containing a backdoor.
impact: Malicious code execution during build or runtime, potential for large-scale 'island-hopping' attacks across thousands of downstream organizations.
prevention: Use Software Bill of Materials (SBOM). Verify digital signatures of all components. Monitor for CVEs in the dependency tree continuously.
defensive_strategy: Strict dependency pinning, automated SBOM analysis, and secure CI/CD pipelines.
secure_coding_checklist: Use a private repository for dependencies.
Review the source code of critical third-party libraries.
Implement integrity checks (hashes) for all fetched binaries.
real_attack_scenario: A state-sponsored actor compromises a build server of a major security vendor, injecting a backdoor into a software update delivered to 18,000 customers.

title: Cryptographic Failures
description: The root cause is often the lack of cryptography or the use of weak/outdated cryptographic algorithms. This often leads to sensitive data exposure.
real_world_example: A web application stores user passwords using the MD5 algorithm without any salt, making it trivial for attackers to decrypt them using rainbow tables.
impact: Disclosure of sensitive data like PII, health records, or credit card information. Loss of data confidentiality and integrity.
prevention: Encrypt all sensitive data at rest and in transit. Use modern, strong algorithms (AES-256, RSA-4096, SHA-3). Disable legacy protocols (TLS 1.0, 1.1).
defensive_strategy: End-to-End Encryption (E2EE), Key Management Systems (KMS), and Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS).
secure_coding_checklist: Never hardcode cryptographic keys in source code.
Use cryptographically secure pseudo-random number generators (CSPRNG).
Ensure HSTS is enforced to prevent Downgrade attacks.
real_attack_scenario: An attacker intercepts unencrypted Wi-Fi traffic and captures session cookies sent over HTTP, allowing them to hijack several executive accounts.

title: Injection
description: Injection flaws, such as SQL, NoSQL, OS, and LDAP injection, occur when untrusted data is sent to an interpreter as part of a command or query.
real_world_example: A search bar on a website directly appends user input to a SQL query, allowing a user to enter `' OR 1=1 --` to bypass login authentication.
impact: Data loss, corruption, or disclosure to unauthorized parties; lack of accountability; or denial of access.
prevention: Use parameterized queries (prepared statements). Use safe APIs that avoid the interpreter entirely. Implement 'allow-list' input validation.
defensive_strategy: Database Least Privilege, WAF (Web Application Firewall) deployment, and Input Sanitization.
secure_coding_checklist: Avoid dynamic query construction.
Escaping special characters for the destination interpreter.
Use LIMIT and other SQL controls to prevent mass data disclosure.
real_attack_scenario: Attacker uses a command injection vulnerability in a file upload utility to execute a reverse shell and gain control of the web server.

title: Insecure Design
description: Focuses on risks related to design flaws. If we want to genuinely 'move left' as an industry, we need more threat modeling, secure design patterns, and reference architectures.
real_world_example: A banking platform implements a password recovery flow that reveals if an email exists in the database, enabling account enumeration attacks.
impact: Fundamental architectural flaws that cannot be fixed by a simple patch; often requires significant code redesign and architectural changes.
prevention: Establish and use a secure development lifecycle (SDLC). Integrate threat modeling into the design phase. Use proven secure design patterns.
defensive_strategy: Threat Modeling (STRIDE/PASTA), Secure-by-Design principles, and Architecture Review Boards.
secure_coding_checklist: Limit retry attempts for sensitive operations.
Ensure business logic flows cannot be bypassed by skipping steps.
Implement segregation of duties in high-risk workflows.
real_attack_scenario: A retailer's e-commerce site allows users to apply an unlimited number of 1% discount codes, eventually resulting in a $0 total for high-value items.

title: Authentication Failures
description: Confirmation of the user's identity, authentication, and session management is critical to protect against authentication-related attacks.
real_world_example: An application allows for unlimited failed login attempts without lockout, permitting an attacker to perform a brute-force attack on a victim's password.
impact: Account hijacking, identity theft, and unauthorized access to user data and administrative functions.
prevention: Implement multi-factor authentication (MFA). Enforce strong password policies. Use industry-standard session management (e.g., OAuth2, OpenID Connect).
business_impact: High (Loss of user data, Compliance failure)
defensive_strategy: Implementation of MFA, adaptive authentication, and secure session management.
secure_coding_checklist: Invalidate sessions on logout.
Use secure, HttpOnly, and SameSite flags for all session cookies.
Implement rate limiting on all authentication endpoints.
real_attack_scenario: Attacker uses 'Credential Stuffing' by trying millions of username/password pairs leaked from other breaches against a corporate login portal.

title: Software or Data Integrity Failures
description: Software or data integrity failures relate to code and infrastructure that does not protect against integrity violations, such as insecure deserialization.
real_world_example: An application accepts a serialized object from the client and deserializes it without validation, allowing the attacker to execute arbitrary code.
impact: Remote code execution (RCE), unauthorized data modification, and persistence in the environment.
prevention: Use digital signatures to verify the integrity of data. Ensure that only trusted sources can provide serialized data. Use safe data formats like JSON.
defensive_strategy: Integrity Monitoring, strict validation of serialized data, and code signing.
secure_coding_checklist: Avoid using native serialization formats.
Implement integrity checks (HMAC) for data sent to the client.
Validate all data after deserialization before use.
real_attack_scenario: Attacker modifies a serialized Java object in a cookie to exploit a known insecure deserialization flaw, gaining an interactive shell on the server.

title: Security Logging and Alerting Failures
description: Failures to log, monitor, and alert on security-critical events can make it difficult for SOC teams to detect and respond to attacks in a timely manner.
real_world_example: A web server log does not record failed login attempts, preventing the security team from detecting a massive brute-force attack until it is too late.
impact: Attackers can remain in the network for extended periods (increased dwell time); inability to perform forensics or meet regulatory requirements.
prevention: Log all authentication failures, access control failures, and input validation errors. Ensure logs are stored securely and centralized. Set up real-time alerts.
defensive_strategy: SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) integration, SOC-as-a-Service, and automated incident response.
secure_coding_checklist: Log user ID, timestamp, and action for all sensitive events.
Do not log sensitive PII or passwords.
Ensure logs are sent to a write-only, centralized logging server.
real_attack_scenario: A threat actor exfiltrates 500GB of database records over 3 months, but because the egress traffic logs were never monitored, the breach goes unnoticed.

title: Mishandling of Exceptional Conditions
description: Inadequate handling of errors and exceptions can lead to information disclosure or allow an attacker to bypass security controls by triggering specific error states.
real_world_example: A database connection error reveals the full internal server path and the specific database driver version in the error message shown to the user.
impact: Information leakage about internal system architecture; potential logic bypasses; application crashes leading to Denial of Service (DoS).
prevention: Implement robust error handling that caught all exceptions. Display generic error messages to users. Log detailed errors on the server side.
defensive_strategy: Graceful Degradation, Error-to-Log mapping, and Exception handling standard frameworks.
secure_coding_checklist: Use 'try-catch-finally' blocks for all resource-intensive operations.
Sanitize all diagnostic information before logging or displaying.
Ensure the application enters a secure state upon failure.
real_attack_scenario: Attacker triggers a 'Division by Zero' error in a pricing script that causes a critical security check to be skipped, granting free access to a service.
